author: @TerrorTubbie666
text:
Did you know AXA HR bicycle-dynamos are crab claw generators too???
(They are called AXA HR in the Netherlands, probably different names in other countries. If you know the name in your country, please add a reply?)

Fun fact:
If you reduce the size of the fingers of the claws to about ⅓ to ½ the original size, you can replace the lightbulbs in the front&rearlight by single LEDs.
You'll see you'll be riding your bike with lights on in daylight, because it's so fr*king funny you don't feel any resistance of the dynamo anymore, while still producing enough electric energy to clearly have your lights on, in daylight! 😂😂😂

replies.author: @TerrorTubbie666
replies.text:
By the way:
There are probably a lot of other brands and types of bicycle dynamos that you can modify the same way to have the same super funny experience.
But I like to use these because axa makes it very simple, I think.

Extra tip:
If there's no screw or nut on top of the wheel of the dynamo, it is pressed on.
Just pull it while wigling it a bit or pry with a flat screwdriver between the wheel and the body of the dynamo.

And you don't need a torxbit for the bottomscrew of the axa. A flat screwdriver will do just fine.

author: @rhiantaylor3446
text: The efficiency of car alternators is generally understood to be poor at about 50%  (despite what Wiki reports of claims from Bosch). I have often wondered what would happen to efficiency if the energising coil were to be replaced by a permanent magnet as you have done. Efficiency is less important than cost in a car application but to capture wind energy I guess we would spend more if we could double the alternator's efficiency and thus output.

author: @icebluscorpion

They did replace the commutator with a induction coil like for charging phones but beefier. No slip rings there. All benefits of a powerful Universal motor without the downside of replacing bruches because there aren't any 😂.

author: @AtlasReburdened
text:
I have an alternator that works like this, but both the field coil and the stator coils are stationary, and the claws simply rotate around the field coil. So there's actually no need for for slip rings or brushes anyway, but apparently the added complexity of that setup makes it cost more than just adding current carrying wear components and knowing that the customer will have to deal with it later. If I recall correctly, the alternator is part of the conversion kit for turning a Ford F350 into an ambulance, so it's designed to be rugged, hence the lack of wear components. It also has a neat feature in that there are extra diodes for the star points, which stray from 0V at some point going up the RPM scale due to the reactance and resistance of the coils. This does the almost magical work of making the coil arrangement star at low rpms, giving the volts needed for equipment at idle, and delta at high rpms, which gives gobs of amps at reduced(but still high enough) volts.
The alternator really is a beautiful piece of engineering, which I'm certain that ford had absolutely nothing to do with😂😂😂.

author: @christopherleubner6633
text: The easiest way to make an alternator self exciting is to carefully drill into the crab claws, only 3 per side, then secure small neodymium magnets in with epoxy ensuring they are installed respecting polarity. When it spins it generates enough current to excite the magnetic exciter regulator without overloading it at lower RPMs. The electromagnet generates a far stronger field than even a rare earth magnet of similar size. ❤

replies.author: @robinhooper7702

author: @TheMadManPlace
text:
Those fancy (albeit powerful) magnets are darn expensive so for a cheaper option use old speaker magnets.
They are mostly free at scrap yards.
What I have often wondered is if you built up the outside of the claws with a welder and then turned them down so that the gap between them and the stator coil assembly was reduced as much as possible would you get a significant increase in output power.
And then taking it to the extreme, grind the inside of the stator assembly plates smooth and concentric to the bearing housings and you could probably get the air gap down to a ridiculously small clearance.
However you might just be creating a siren.

replies.author: @christopherleubner6633
replies.text: The ones from microwave ovens are free as well, and have a much stronger field and are a perfect fit on the alternator shaft. I just carefully drill three of the claws on each side enough to fit a small RE magnet into it then set it in place with JB weld. It gives enough energy to self start the alternator allowing it to operate at far lower RPMs.❤

replies.text: Yup those make awsome windmill generators. Just use a 3 phase diode (6 diodes in 3 pairs back to back. Makes 24V at about two amps with moderate turning. author: @vylbird8014
text: Why magnets? The great advantage of a field coil is that you can use feedback to regulate the current, and thus the output voltage. And the field coil goes on the rotor so the slip rings only have to handle the comparatively low current.

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: If you do decide to modify an alternator it would be a good idea to replace the shafts with a stainless steel or other non magnetic material.  Reason is that it is magnetic and will buy pass some of the magnetic field resulting in a weaker field at the fingers. This is a lesson I learnt when I did this experiment.  I got 240 W at 8000rpm with the original shafts and 1200W at 8000rpm when I put in a non magnetic shaft. If you have a magnetic field strength meter you can actually measure the difference.

replies.author: @ogi22
replies.text: Very good tip! I sometimes wondered if there is some magnetic flux losses due to the shaft. Now you got me an answer, thank you 🥰

replies.author: @justtinkering6713
replies.text: I'd like to see a wind turbine survive 8000 rpm, yikes! I still like axial flux because the fingers have a reluctance and distance from the coil center matters, ie: inverse square law. So still thunderbolt 3 phase for me, 12 magnets and 9 coils.

replies.author: @bartronicsecurity
replies.text: @ogi22 Just remember one thing. Putting in a maget in the rotor does make it harder to start turning initially as magnetic coging takes place. But once the rotor starts it works like a flywheel.  This coging gets harder and more noticable with a non magnetic  stainless steel shaft than with the original magnetic shaft which makes sense.

replies.author: @ogi22
replies.text: @justtinkering6713 That's what gear box is for 😉

replies.author: @ogi22
replies.text: @bartronicsecurity It does make sense. If much more field is directed to claws, then it should be normal.
